threats.title: First Claimer Steals All Accumulated Pool Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim` function collects all accumulated fees for the entire shared Uniswap V3 position and sends the excess (belonging to other users) to the `FEE_TAKER`.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct violation of fee distribution fairness. The first user to claim will cause all other users' fees to be swept to the fee taker, and subsequent claims will revert due to underflow when trying to deduct the user's expected fee share from the now-empty collected amount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.collect(..., type(uint128).max, type(uint128).max) sweeps all fees

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First claimer triggers collection of all position fees, not just their share
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Fee taker receives windfall of other users' fees; subsequent users lose their fees and cannot claim principal

economic_questions: Does the underflow permanently lock subsequent users' principal?
Can an attacker intentionally trigger this to grief the pool or collude with the fee taker?
validation_requirements: Simulate multiple users claiming after trading fees accumulate
Verify if subsequent claims revert due to insufficient contract balance or underflow

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (just enough to buy a small position and pay gas)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of accumulated pool fees redirected to FEE_TAKER
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High for FEE_TAKER, 100% loss for late claimers

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires active trading on the Uniswap V3 pool to generate fees
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a period of high trading volume
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Destroys investor confidence and locks user funds

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All investors who do not claim first
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of accumulated fees, potential 100% loss of principal if claim reverts
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire ILO pool liquidity and fees

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check fee deduction math and contract token balances post-claim
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify collect(max) behavior on shared positions

threats.title: Whitelist Logic Flaw Allows Universal Access
threats.hypothesis_description: The whitelist check logic `_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` evaluates to false for unwhitelisted users when `_allowedWhitelistIndex > 0`, completely bypassing the revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unwhitelisted users have a default index of 0. If the allowed index is set to any value greater than 0, `0 > allowed` is false, and the `NotWhitelisted` revert is skipped. This allows anyone to buy tokens, defeating the core purpose of the whitelist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Flawed boolean logic in whitelist index check

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Unwhitelisted users (index 0) do not trigger the revert when allowedIndex > 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attackers can bypass whitelist restrictions to snipe tokens during the protected launch phase

economic_questions: Can bots completely buy out the supply before legitimate whitelisted users?
Does this break the intended token distribution model?
validation_requirements: Test purchasing with an unwhitelisted address when _allowedWhitelistIndex is 1

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Arbitrage profit from early token access
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on token price action post-launch

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires Uniswap V3 pool to be active

threats.title: Per-User Cap Bypass via NFT Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: Users can bypass the `maxCapPerUser` limit by transferring their ILOPool NFT to another address, resetting their `balanceOf` to 0 and allowing them to mint a new NFT with a fresh cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The cap is enforced per-NFT-position rather than per-address historically. By moving the NFT to a cold wallet, a user can repeatedly buy up to the max cap until the global hard cap is reached, monopolizing the sale.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Cap check relies on current NFT position raiseAmount

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transferring the NFT resets balanceOf to 0, creating a new position with 0 raiseAmount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can accumulate unlimited tokens, dominating the ILO and dumping on retail later

economic_questions: How quickly can an attacker drain the hard cap?
What is the gas overhead of the NFT transfer loop?
validation_requirements: Test buy -> transfer -> buy sequence in a single block

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for multiple buy and transfer calls

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Centralizes token ownership, defeating fair launch

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other investors who are crowded out

threats.title: Oracle Manipulation for ETH Contribution Cap Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: The TWAP oracle falls back to the oldest available observation if the pool is newer than the 30-minute period. This makes the TWAP act as a spot price, which can be manipulated via flash loans to bypass the ETH contribution cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can manipulate the Uniswap V3 pool spot price to make VULT extremely cheap in ETH terms. The oracle will return this manipulated price, making a massive VULT purchase appear to cost very little ETH, bypassing the `_maxAddressCap`.
preconditions: Oracle pool is newly created (< 30 mins old)
Attacker has capital to manipulate spot price

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint32 period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod;
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Short period allows spot price manipulation to dictate TWAP
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys massive amounts of VULT while the contract thinks they are under the 3 ETH cap

economic_questions: How much capital is required to move the spot price sufficiently?
Can this be executed atomically in a single block?
validation_requirements: Simulate flash loan manipulation on a fresh Uniswap V3 pool
Calculate resulting estimated ETH amount in Whitelist contract

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Flash loan fees and swap slippage
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Massive token accumulation at launch prices

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test TWAP response to single-block manipulation

threats.title: Direct Transfers Bypass Whitelist and Blacklist
threats.hypothesis_description: The `checkWhitelist` function only enforces restrictions if the tokens are bought directly from the Uniswap pool (`from == _pool`). Direct ERC20 transfers bypass all whitelist and blacklist checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: A whitelisted user can buy tokens and immediately transfer them to a blacklisted or unwhitelisted address. The `_beforeTokenTransfer` hook triggers `checkWhitelist`, but since `from != _pool`, the checks are skipped, defeating the token transfer restrictions.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Direct transfers do not originate from _pool, bypassing the block
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Blacklisted entities or unwhitelisted whales can acquire tokens via OTC deals with whitelisted users

economic_questions: Does this completely invalidate the regulatory/compliance purpose of the whitelist?
Can a secondary market be established immediately?
validation_requirements: Test transferring tokens from a whitelisted buyer to a blacklisted address

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All tokens held by whitelisted users

threats.title: Missing Upper Bound Check on Initial Pool Price
threats.hypothesis_description: The `initILOPool` function fails to check if `initialPoolPriceX96` is less than `sqrtRatioUpperX96`. If the price is set above the upper tick, liquidity calculations will be fundamentally broken.
threats.investigation_rationale: Without the upper bound check, a project admin can set an initial price outside the tick range. This causes `_saleAmountNeeded` to calculate incorrect liquidity amounts, potentially leading to a failed launch or severe mispricing of investor positions.
preconditions: Project admin initializes pool with price >= upper tick

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Price outside range causes LiquidityAmounts to swap bounds, breaking math
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Investors buy positions with miscalculated liquidity, leading to stolen funds or locked assets

economic_questions: What exact liquidity values are produced when price is out of bounds?
Does this allow an attacker to drain the SALE_TOKEN during launch?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with out-of-bounds price and simulate buy/launch flow

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of funds due to bad math

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test LiquidityAmounts behavior with swapped bounds
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate exact token amounts required vs deposited

threats.title: Third-Party Griefing of Whitelist Allocation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `buy` function allows anyone to specify a `recipient`. An attacker can spend their own funds to buy tokens on behalf of a whitelisted user, filling their `maxCapPerUser` allocation without their consent.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the attacker loses funds, they can permanently lock a victim out of participating in the ILO by exhausting their cap. This is a practical griefing vector against high-profile or competing investors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.sender pays but recipient gets cap exhausted

preconditions: Victim is whitelisted and hasn't reached cap

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls buy(maxCap, victim) to fill victim's cap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but eliminates competition for limited token supply

economic_questions: Is the cost of griefing worth the competitive advantage?
Can this be used to force a specific distribution of tokens?
validation_requirements: Verify that a third party can successfully call buy for another address

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas + cost of tokens (which attacker loses to victim)

threats.title: Zero-Duration Vesting Causes Permanent Claim DOS
threats.hypothesis_description: If a vesting schedule is configured with `start == end`, the `_unlockedLiquidity` calculation will divide by zero, permanently locking all claims for that position.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_validateVestSchedule` function does not enforce `start < end`. If a project admin accidentally or maliciously sets a zero-duration vest, the denominator `(vest.end - vest.start) * BPS` becomes zero, causing `FullMath.mulDiv` to revert.
deployment_assumptions: Project admin configures a vesting schedule with start == end
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided for vesting schedules, depends on runtime input.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Zero denominator causes revert in FullMath.mulDiv
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Malicious project admin can lock investor funds permanently

economic_questions: Can this be used to extort investors?
Is there any recovery path once initialized?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with start==end and attempt to claim

threats.title: Liquidity Accounting Mismatch Locks Value
threats.hypothesis_description: Rounding differences between per-user liquidity calculations in `buy()` and aggregate liquidity calculation in `launch()` cause the sum of user liquidities to be less than the actual Uniswap position liquidity, permanently locking the remainder.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because `buy()` uses floor division for each user's liquidity share, and `launch()` calculates the total liquidity from `totalRaised`, accumulated rounding errors lead to a mismatch. The tracked liquidity is less than the actual Uniswap liquidity, leaving a portion of the position permanently unclaimable.
preconditions: Many users participate in the sale

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Sum of floored individual liquidities vs floored total liquidity
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Value is permanently locked in the Uniswap V3 position
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but represents a continuous value leak for the protocol

economic_questions: What is the maximum theoretical value locked per pool?
Does this affect fee collection?
validation_requirements: Simulate 1000 users buying small amounts and compare sum to total launch liquidity

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: High number of participants exacerbates the issue

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative rounding errors across all pools

threats.title: Refund Deadline Modification Permanently Locks Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The ILOManager owner can arbitrarily change the `refundDeadline` to `type(uint64).max`, permanently blocking the refund path for all investors if a project fails to launch.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a project fails to launch (e.g., due to soft cap not met or price manipulation), investors rely on the refund mechanism. A compromised or malicious owner can lock these funds forever by pushing the deadline infinitely into the future.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner sets deadline to max uint64, preventing refundable() modifier from passing
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extortion of investors or project admins

economic_questions: Is there any timelock on this owner action?
Can this be used to force a launch under unfavorable conditions?
validation_requirements: Verify owner can call this during an active sale

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool fails to meet soft cap
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of trust and locked funds

threats.title: Unbounded Fee Configuration Causes Claim Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The ILOManager owner can set `PLATFORM_FEE` or `PERFORMANCE_FEE` greater than 10000 BPS (100%). This causes the `_deductFees` function to underflow, reverting all user claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: Without an upper bound check, a malicious or compromised owner can brick the entire claim process for newly initialized projects by setting fees above 100%, causing a silent underflow in Solidity 0.7.6.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: feeBPS > 10000 causes subtrahend to be larger than amount0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing or extortion by locking claims

economic_questions: Does this affect already initialized projects?
Can the owner fix it after the fact?
validation_requirements: Test setting fee to 10001 and calling claim
